title: Activision Blizzard acquisition for $68.7 billion
searchableText:
Activision Blizzard acquisition for $68.7 billion
Should Microsoft acquire Activision Blizzard for $95 per share in an all-cash transaction?
Microsoft has agreed to acquire Activision Blizzard for $68.7 billion inclusive of net cash at $95 per share.
Expand gaming portfolio and content library
Both boards of directors have approved the transaction
All-cash transaction structure
{"description":"Complete the $68.7 billion all-cash acquisition","expectedBenefit":"Acquire Activision Blizzard's portfolio at $95 per share","id":"proceed","principalRisk":"Regulatory scrutiny of a large gaming merger","selected":true}
{"description":"Withdraw the acquisition offer","expectedBenefit":"Avoid potential antitrust and regulatory challenges","id":"reject","principalRisk":"Loss of strategic content and portfolio growth","selected":false}
proceed
Avoidance of regulatory scrutiny
Shareholder and financial information, as well as today’s 6:00 a.m. Pacific time conference call with investors and analysts, is available at http://www.microsoft.com/en-us/investor.
Microsoft will acquire Activision Blizzard for $95.00 per share, in an all-cash transaction valued at $68.7 billion, inclusive of Activision Blizzard’s net cash.
We’ve agreed to acquire Activision Blizzard for $95 per share in an all-cash transaction valued at $68.7 billion, inclusive of Activision Blizzard’s net cash.
The transaction has been approved by the boards of directors of both Microsoft and Activision Blizzard.

title: Scale Azure AI infrastructure while constraining operating expense
searchableText:
Scale Azure AI infrastructure while constraining operating expense
How should Microsoft scale infrastructure and operating resources as demand and profitability constraints change?
Azure AI infrastructure required a material capacity increase while Microsoft was aligning costs with revenue growth and protecting profitability.
Lead the AI platform wave
Maintain healthy profitability while scaling capacity
Azure AI infrastructure required material sequential investment.
Scaled capital investment would increase COGS.
FY24 operating-expense growth was expected to remain low.
Capacity buildout timing varies by quarter.
{"description":"Scale AI infrastructure and operating resources together.","expectedBenefit":"Maximizes organizational capacity around the platform shift.","id":"scale_infrastructure_and_people","principalRisk":"Weakens operating leverage while infrastructure COGS is rising.","selected":false}
{"description":"Increase Azure AI infrastructure capital while keeping operating-expense growth low.","expectedBenefit":"Funds urgent capacity while preserving disciplined profitability.","id":"scale_infrastructure_constrain_opex","principalRisk":"Requires aggressive internal resource prioritization.","selected":true}
scale_infrastructure_constrain_opex
Expanding people with infrastructure could reduce execution bottlenecks.
We expect capital expenditures to have a material sequential increase on a dollar basis driven by investments in Azure AI infrastructure.
As always, we remain committed to aligning costs and revenue growth to deliver disciplined profitability. Therefore, while the scaled capex investments will impact COGS growth, we expect FY24 operating expense growth to remain low.

title: Accelerate demand-led AI capacity with disciplined cost and mixed supply
searchableText:
Accelerate demand-led AI capacity with disciplined cost and mixed supply
How should Microsoft scale infrastructure and operating resources as demand and profitability constraints change?
Customer demand and pipeline required faster cloud and AI capacity additions than owned buildout alone could always deliver.
Add capacity in time to serve visible customer demand
Preserve operating-margin discipline while scaling AI investment
Customer demand guided cloud and AI investment.
Third-party capacity supplemented owned capacity when lead time required it.
AI capital investment increases COGS.
Every team remained subject to disciplined cost management.
{"description":"Scale owned infrastructure and operating headcount together.","expectedBenefit":"Keeps execution resources under direct organizational control.","id":"scale_infrastructure_and_people","principalRisk":"Adds recurring operating cost while capital intensity is rising.","selected":false}
{"description":"Accelerate infrastructure, mix owned and third-party supply, and retain operating-cost discipline.","expectedBenefit":"Responds faster to pipeline without matching CapEx growth with broad OpEx growth.","id":"scale_infrastructure_constrain_opex","principalRisk":"External capacity may have different economics or control characteristics.","selected":true}
scale_infrastructure_constrain_opex
Scaling people alongside owned capacity could increase direct control and reduce external dependency.
Our commitment to scaling our cloud and AI investment is guided by customer demand and the substantial market opportunity. As we scale these investments, we remain focused on driving efficiencies across every layer of our tech stack and disciplined cost management across every team.
I feel like primarily, obviously, this is being built by us, but we’ve also used third-party capacity to help when we could have that help us, in terms of meeting customer demand. And I tend to think, looking forward, you’ll tend to see, and I guided toward it, accelerating capital expense to continue to be able to add capacity in the coming quarters, given what we see in terms of pipeline.

title: Slow or pause selected early-stage datacenter projects
searchableText:
Slow or pause selected early-stage datacenter projects
When should Microsoft pace or pause early-stage AI datacenter projects while retaining the broader infrastructure strategy?
A multi-year capital program had created projects across locations before final customer demand, adoption, and business-priority signals were fully observable.
Align infrastructure timing and location with customer demand
Preserve the strategic AI capacity program without committing every early-stage project
Some projects remain early-stage and comparatively reversible.
Updated demand, adoption, or business-priority signals no longer support the original timing or location.
Microsoft must continue serving growing cloud and AI demand.
The adjustment must not be treated as abandonment of the global AI strategy.
{"description":"Continue every previously planned datacenter project on its original schedule.","expectedBenefit":"Maximizes nominal future capacity and avoids schedule disruption.","id":"continue_all_planned_projects","principalRisk":"Locks capital into locations or timing no longer justified by demand.","selected":false}
{"description":"Slow or pause selected early-stage projects while continuing the core global capacity program.","expectedBenefit":"Preserves flexibility and aligns irreversible capital with current demand signals.","id":"slow_or_pause_early_stage_projects","principalRisk":"Capacity may arrive late if demand accelerates unexpectedly.","selected":true}
slow_or_pause_early_stage_projects
Continuing every project would maximize planned capacity and schedule certainty.
What this means is that we are slowing or pausing some early-stage projects.
these expenditures are dependent on demand signals and adoption of our services that will be managed through the year

title: 2013 forty billion dollar share repurchase authorization
searchableText:

The board of directors is considering approving a new share repurchase program.
Return cash to shareholders.
Signal commitment to shareholder value.
The board of directors must approve the program.
The authorization limit is set at $40 billion.
The action must reflect a continued commitment to shareholders.
The decision is being made on September 17, 2013.

title: 2024 sixty billion dollar share repurchase authorization
searchableText:

Return cash to shareholders.

Enhance shareholder value.

title: Reduce actual buyback deployment while preserving authorization flexibility
searchableText:
Reduce actual buyback deployment while preserving authorization flexibility
When should Microsoft reduce actual share-repurchase deployment despite continuing shareholder-return commitments and available authorization?
Microsoft retained authorization capacity and a shareholder-return commitment while facing competing strategic investment and capital-flexibility needs.
Preserve a credible shareholder cash-return commitment
Retain flexibility for higher-return internal investment and liquidity needs
The authorization is a ceiling rather than a required deployment amount.
Competing strategic investment or flexibility has higher marginal value than immediate repurchases.
The dividend and long-term shareholder-return commitment remain intact.
Observed deployment data does not disclose the full contemporaneous causal explanation.

reduce_buyback_deployment_preserve_flexibility

title: Nuance healthcare cloud acquisition for 19.7 billion dollars
searchableText:
Nuance healthcare cloud acquisition for 19.7 billion dollars
Should Microsoft proceed with the acquisition of Nuance for $19.7 billion?
Microsoft announced a definitive agreement to acquire Nuance for $56.00 per share.
Acquire Nuance for $19.7 billion.
Create value through terrific execution.
The merger agreement must be adopted by Nuance stockholders.
Certain governmental and regulatory approvals must be received.
The transaction is an all-cash deal.
The acquisition is valued at $19.7 billion inclusive of net debt.
{"description":"Proceed with the acquisition as agreed.","expectedBenefit":"Realize value creation through strong execution.","id":"option-1","principalRisk":"Failure to satisfy consummation conditions.","selected":true}
{"description":"Re-evaluate the acquisition terms or halt the deal.","expectedBenefit":"Mitigate risks associated with integration or execution.","id":"option-2","principalRisk":"Missing the opportunity to acquire Nuance.","selected":false}
Proceed with the acquisition as agreed.
Mitigating risks associated with integration or execution.
And with all acquisitions, value was ultimately only created with terrific execution, bind aligned team with a shared vision of the future.
n stock of Nuance, (ii) the failure to satisfy the conditions to the consummation of the transaction, including the adoption of the merger agreement by the stockholders of Nuance and the receipt of certain governmental and regulatory approvals, (iii) the occurrence of any event, change or other circumstance that could give rise to the termination of the merger agreement, (iv) the effect of the announcement or pendency of the transaction on Nuance’s business relationships, operating results, and business generally, (v) risks that the proposed transaction disrupts current plans and operations of Nuance or Microsoft and potential difficulties in Nuance employee retention as a result of the transaction, (vi) risks related to diverting management’s attention from Nuance’s ongoing business operations, (vii) the outcome of any legal proceedings that may be instituted against us or against Nuance related to the merger agreement or the transaction, (viii) the ability of Microsoft to successfully integrate Nuance’s operations, product lines, and technology, and (ix) the ability of Microsoft to implement its plans, forecasts, and other expectations with respect to Nuance’s business after the completion of the proposed merger and realize additional opportunities for growth and innovation.
the occurrence of any event, change or other circumstance that could give rise to the termination of the merger agreement
Microsoft Corp (Nasdaq: MSFT) and Nuance Communications, Inc. (Nasdaq: NUAN) today announced they have entered into a definitive agreement under which Microsoft will acquire Nuance for $56.00 per share, implying a 23% premium to the closing price of Nuance on Friday, April 9, in an all-cash transaction valued at $19.7 billion, inclusive of Nuance’s net debt.
Microsoft Corp (Nasdaq: MSFT) and Nuance Communications, Inc. (Nasdaq: NUAN) today announced they have entered into a definitive agreement under which Microsoft will acquire Nuance for $56.00 per share, implying a 23% premium to the closing price of Nuance on Friday, April 9, in an all-cash transaction valued at $19.7 billion, inclusive of Nuance’s net debt.

title: Expand operating resources in high-growth differentiated areas
searchableText:
Expand operating resources in high-growth differentiated areas
When should Microsoft expand operating expense and headcount instead of retaining savings or constraining resources?
Microsoft entered FY2022 with strong growth opportunities and operating savings, requiring a choice between preserving cost restraint and reinvesting in differentiated growth areas.
Fund high-growth opportunities customers value
Preserve confidence in long-term margin execution
The target area has observable high growth.
Microsoft offers differentiation customers care about.
Prior investment execution supports confidence in returns.
Operating investment must remain concentrated rather than uniform.
Growth in expense and headcount must be justified by opportunity and execution.

searchableText:

Should the company unbundle Teams from M365/O365 suites in the EEA and Switzerland, offering them at a lower price?
The company is considering changes to its suite pricing structure in the EEA and Switzerland.
Offer M365/O365 without Teams at a lower price.
Maintain or enhance perceived value despite the unbundling.
The unbundling will occur in the EEA and Switzerland.
The lower price will be €2 less per month or €24 per year.
The offering must be a standalone suite without Teams.
The price reduction must be applied to the base suite.

title: Microsoft eliminating 4800 roles in Commercial and Xbox transformation
searchableText:
Microsoft eliminating 4800 roles in Commercial and Xbox transformation
Should Microsoft proceed with eliminating approximately 4,800 roles to focus resources on key priorities?
Microsoft is eliminating around 4,800 roles, representing about 2.1% of its global workforce.
Focus people, investments, and energy on key priorities.
Ensure headcount is used in the most productive way possible.
The elimination is part of the Commercial and Xbox transformation.
Microsoft has added significant headcount over the past 12 months.
The action must align with investing in focused areas.
The reduction must be targeted to maximize productivity.
{"description":"Proceed with eliminating the 4,800 roles as planned.","expectedBenefit":"Better alignment of resources to high-priority areas.","id":"option-1","principalRisk":"Potential disruption or morale impact from workforce reduction.","selected":true}
{"description":"Reduce the scope of elimination, targeting fewer than 4,800 roles.","expectedBenefit":"Mitigate workforce disruption while still achieving focus.","id":"option-2","principalRisk":"Failing to achieve the necessary level of resource focus.","selected":false}
Proceed with eliminating the 4,800 roles as planned.
Mitigating workforce disruption by reducing the scope of elimination.
It’ll be about investing where we said we would invest, which is in focused areas. It’s about moving headcount to make sure, inside the company, we’ve got it on the highest and most important things. We’ve added a lot of headcount over the past 12 months, and we want to make sure we use those headcount in the most productive way possible.
Today we are eliminating around 4,800 roles, about 2.1% of our global workforce, as we focus our people, investments, and energy on the priorities that will keep Microsoft positioned

title: Ten thousand role workforce and cost structure reset
searchableText:
Ten thousand role workforce and cost structure reset
How should we realign our cost structure and talent allocation in response to customers optimizing digital spend and the emergence of AI computing?
Customers are doing more with less and some regions face recession; simultaneously, AI is creating a new computing platform, requiring a shift in where capital and talent are deployed.
Align cost structure with revenue and customer demand
Reallocate talent to secular growth and long-term competitiveness areas
Deliver ongoing results while investing in long-term opportunity
Some parts of the world are in or anticipating recession
The next major computing wave is being born with advances in AI
Must remain a consequential company in an unforgiving industry
Need to adapt to platform shifts throughout the company's history
{"description":"Reduce overall workforce by 10,000 roles through FY23 Q3 while continuing to hire in key strategic areas and investing in AI.","expectedBenefit":"Aligns cost structure with revenue, moves headcount to highest-productivity areas, and positions the company for the AI computing wave.","id":"reduce_and_reallocate","principalRisk":"Severance and portfolio changes create a $1.2 billion charge in Q2; potential loss of institutional knowledge in eliminated roles.","selected":true}
{"description":"Maintain current workforce levels and expand hiring across all departments to capture every possible customer opportunity without role reductions.","expectedBenefit":"Avoids severance costs and retains all existing talent and institutional knowledge.","id":"maintain_and_expand","principalRisk":"Cost structure becomes unaligned with revenue; talent is spread thin across low-growth areas instead of focused strategic areas.","selected":false}
reduce_and_reallocate
Avoidance of severance costs and retention of all institutional knowledge
First, as we saw customers accelerate their digital spend during the pandemic, we’re now seeing them optimize their digital spend to do more with less. We’re also seeing organizations in every industry and geography exercise caution as some parts of the world are in a recession and other parts are anticipating one. At the same time, the next major wave of computing is being born with advances in AI, as we’re turning the world’s most advanced models into a new computing platform. This is the context in which we as a company must strive to deliver results on an ongoing basis, while investing in our long-term opportunity.
Second, we will continue to invest in strategic areas for our future, meaning we are allocating both our capital and talent to areas of secular growth and long-term competitiveness for the company, while divesting in other areas. These are the kinds of hard choices we have made throughout our 47-year history to remain a consequential company in this industry that is unforgiving to anyone who doesn’t adapt to platform shifts. As such, we are taking a $1.2 billion charge in Q2 related to severance costs, changes to our hardware portfolio, and the cost of lease consolidation as we create higher density across our workspaces.
First, we will align our cost structure with our revenue and where we see customer demand. Today, we are making changes that will result in the reduction of our overall workforce by 10,000 jobs through the end of FY23 Q3. This represents less than 5 percent of our total employee base, with some notifications happening today. It’s important to note that while we are eliminating roles in some areas, we will continue to hire in key strategic areas.
It’ll be about investing where we said we would invest, which is in focused areas. It’s about moving headcount to make sure, inside the company, we’ve got it on the highest and most important things. We’ve added a lot of headcount over the past 12 months, and we want to make sure we use those headcount in the most productive way possible.

title: cost_efficiency: Reduce or reallocate resources toward the highest-priority work while preserving focused investment.
searchableText:
cost_efficiency

Strategic priority alignment
Customer-valued differentiation and growth
Workforce productivity
Cost and revenue alignment
Execution continuity
Reduce or reallocate resources toward the highest-priority work while preserving focused investment.
A verified high-growth opportunity has customer-valued differentiation and prior execution supports additional investment.
Judge cost discipline by resource placement and return evidence rather than imposing uniform cuts.
Preserve execution continuity while changing headcount or cost structure.
Expand operating expense and headcount selectively when growth, differentiation, and execution evidence jointly support the investment.
Priority alignment improves, reductions impair a verified growth opportunity, or customer-valued differentiation and execution evidence justify renewed expansion.
When should Microsoft reduce or reallocate resources, and when should it expand operating investment instead?
The 2023 and 2026 events reduce or reallocate resources when deployment is misaligned with priorities, while the FY2022 plan expands headcount and operating investment where growth and customer-valued differentiation are verified.
Allocate resources toward the highest-priority productive opportunity; cost discipline governs placement and return evidence rather than imposing uniform cuts.
Reduce or reallocate when current deployment is below strategic priority or productivity thresholds.
Expand selectively when growth, customer-valued differentiation, and prior execution support the investment.

We’ve added a lot of headcount over the past 12 months, and we want to make sure we use those headcount in the most productive way possible.
Today we are eliminating around 4,800 roles, about 2.1% of our global workforce, as we focus our people, investments, and energy on the priorities that will keep Microsoft positioned

title: shareholder_return_risk: Authorize bounded repurchase capacity while retaining discretion over actual deployment.
searchableText:
shareholder_return_risk
A continuing cash-return commitment is supportable within an explicit authorization ceiling and capital flexibility remains adequate.
Strategic investment return
Liquidity and capital flexibility
Durable dividend commitment
Opportunistic share repurchase
Authorization ceiling
Authorize bounded repurchase capacity while retaining discretion over actual deployment.
Higher-return strategic investment, liquidity needs, or unattractive repurchase economics dominate immediate buyback deployment.
Treat the authorization as a ceiling rather than a requirement to deploy the full amount.
Preserve the long-term shareholder-return commitment while varying the more flexible buyback lever.
Reduce actual buyback deployment while preserving authorization when strategic investment or capital flexibility has higher marginal value.
Liquidity tightens, higher-return investment needs emerge, repurchase economics deteriorate, or authorization deployment would constrain strategic flexibility.
When should Microsoft authorize and deploy shareholder cash return, and when should it reduce actual buyback deployment?
The 2013 and 2024 events preserve bounded repurchase capacity and a shareholder-return commitment, while FY2023 shows materially lower actual deployment despite available authorization.
Preserve the long-term shareholder-return commitment while treating buyback deployment as an opportunistic and reversible capital-allocation lever.
Authorize and deploy when capital flexibility is adequate and repurchase economics remain attractive.
Reduce deployment when higher-return investment, liquidity, or flexibility needs dominate.

When should AI infrastructure scale, and when should selected early-stage capacity projects be slowed or paused?
The 2023 and 2024 events scale infrastructure under visible demand and capacity urgency, while the 2025 event slows or pauses selected early-stage projects when updated demand, adoption, and business-priority signals change the required timing or location.
Keep the AI capacity strategy demand-led and preserve flexibility by changing the most reversible asset commitments first.
Scale when customer demand, capacity urgency, and infrastructure economics remain supportable.
Pace or pause early-stage projects when location, timing, adoption, or business-priority signals weaken.

As always, we remain committed to aligning costs and revenue growth to deliver disciplined profitability. Therefore, while the scaled capex investments will impact COGS growth, we expect FY24 operating expense growth to remain low.
Our commitment to scaling our cloud and AI investment is guided by customer demand and the substantial market opportunity. As we scale these investments, we remain focused on driving efficiencies across every layer of our tech stack and disciplined cost management across every team.
